title: Add a Degraded state to pods
body:
Make a distinction between pods that are completely running (all containers running) and those that have some containers going, but not all, by introducing an intermediate state between Stopped and Running called Degraded. A Degraded pod has at least one, but not all, containers running; a Running pod has all containers running.

First step to a solution for #7213.

Looks to be going green, @containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

Could you add a test for this?

Setup/run a pod with two containers.
Get the pod state.
Stop on of the containers
Get the pod state
Stop the last container
Get the pod state.

> I think the description w.r.t. "running" are not 100 percent accurate. To me, "running" implies that the container is running and has not stopped. But in case of a pod, some container may have finished already (and hence stopped). Would "all containers have been started" be more accurate?

@mheon can you reply? If I start a pod with two containers, and one of them exits, is the pod's state "degraded"?

Yes. I want a clear distinction between all running and partially running.

If you want to make a further distinction between all running and some
naturally stopped (versus never started) we might could add another state,
though I have some concerns about how we'd distinguish (we don't have the
exit code at this point so I can't tell if the container exited cleanly,
and I don't think we can actually tell if the container exited on this run
of the pod and not the last time the pod was ran).

On Thu, Oct 22, 2020, 03:33 Valentin Rothberg <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> me, "running" implies that the container is running and has not stopped.
> But in case of a pod, some container may have finished already (and hence
> stopped). Would "all containers have been started" be more accurate?

> @mheon <https://github.com/mheon> can you reply? If I start a pod with

@mheon, thanks!

> If you want to make a further distinction between all running and some
naturally stopped (versus never started) we might could add another state,

I _think_ that counting which containers have been started would be more accurate. Would it?

My concern is what happens after the first time the pod is started and stopped - after that, all we have is a mix of Exited and Running, and it becomes very difficult to tell what's going on (did the user start one or two container but not the whole pod? Did the user start the whole pod, but half of it exited? We really can't tell, because the containers start in Exited state from the first time the pod was run, and end in Exited state after they finish).
